content.claim: Rising SEN identification may change future educational trajectories and access (e.g., tracking into vocational streams, secondary-school access, higher-education pathways) through changes in SNI status from historical advantage to current frequent exclusion in admissions, but longitudinal quantification of these distributional outcomes is absent from retrieved sources and represents a priority evidence gap.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Equity and social mobility lens
limitations: Claim that SNI status functions as exclusionary factor is sourced to pedagogical journal and lacks quantified admissions-outcome data
no longitudinal study follows SNI-classified cohorts to secondary/tertiary educational outcomes or labor-market trajectories
temporal sequence of when SNI status shifted from advantage to exclusion is not precisely dated across regions
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
